q: On what principle does fibre optics work?
How does fibre optics transmit information?
a: as light pulses along a glass or plastic fibre
as electrical signals through copper wires

q: How many glass fibres can a fibre optic cable contain?
a: from a few up to hundreds

q: What are the two concentric layers of an optical fibre called?
What surrounds the glass fibre core in an optical fibre?
What surrounds the cladding in an optical fibre?
What is the purpose of the sheath (plastic buffer) in an optical fibre?
How do the refractive indices of the core and cladding compare?
a: The refractive index of core will be slightly greater than that of cladding
The refractive index of cladding will be slightly greater than that of core

q: Under what condition does total internal reflection occur in an optical fibre?
a: if the light ray is injected and strikes the core to cladding interface at an angle greater than the critical angle
if the light ray strikes at an angle less than the critical angle
if the light ray is absorbed by the cladding

q: What happens if the angle of incidence at the core to cladding interface is less than the critical angle?
a: both reflection and refraction take place

q: What causes the light beam to attenuate and die off over a certain distance if the angle of incidence is less than the critical angle?
What is a useful application of fibre optics in medicine?

a: a non-surgical procedure to examine the interior of a hollow organ or cavity of the human body
a surgical procedure to remove tumors
a diagnostic test using external radiation

q: How do endoscopes use optical fibres?
a: to observe an image inside the body

q: What is inserted into the body to monitor interior organs clearly using fibre optics?
a: a camera with a conventional lens
